steps.title: Step 1 — draw each particle separately with its own forces
steps.body: Sketch particle A (on the table) and particle B (hanging) as two separate diagrams. Mark every force on each: for A, weight $m_Ag$, normal reaction $N$, friction $F$, and tension $T$; for B, weight $m_Bg$ and tension $T$. The string is the *only* thing connecting the two diagrams.

steps.title: Step 2 — fix a positive direction and use the same a for both
steps.body: Because the string is **light and inextensible**, it cannot stretch, so both particles move with the **same magnitude of acceleration** $a$ at every instant, and the **same tension** $T$ acts throughout the string (a light, frictionless pulley just redirects the string without changing the tension). Choose the direction each particle actually moves in as positive for that particle — here, A moves toward the pulley and B moves downward.

steps.title: Step 3 — write F = ma for particle A (on the table)
steps.body: Perpendicular to the table: $N = m_Ag$ (no vertical acceleration). Along the table, taking the direction of motion as positive: $$T - \mu N = m_A a \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T - \mu m_A g = m_A a.$$ Friction opposes A's motion, so it acts *away* from the pulley, subtracting from $T$.

steps.title: Step 4 — write F = ma for particle B (hanging)
steps.body: B has no horizontal forces; vertically, taking downward (its direction of motion) as positive: $$m_Bg - T = m_B a.$$ Weight drives the motion down; tension resists it, pulling B back up towards the pulley.

steps.title: Step 5 — eliminate T by adding the two equations
steps.body: Adding the equation for A, $T - \mu m_A g = m_A a$, to the equation for B, $m_Bg - T = m_B a$, the tensions cancel: $$m_Bg - \mu m_Ag = (m_A + m_B)a \quad\Longrightarrow\quad a = \frac{m_Bg - \mu m_Ag}{m_A+m_B}.$$ This is the same trick used for a frictionless Atwood machine, with the friction term $\mu m_A g$ simply subtracted from the driving weight $m_Bg$.

steps.title: Step 6 — back-substitute to find T
steps.body: Once $a$ is known, substitute into **either** original equation to find $T$ — using both as a check is good practice. From B's equation: $T = m_Bg - m_Ba = m_B(g-a)$. From A's equation: $T = m_Aa + \mu m_Ag = m_A(a+\mu g)$. Both must give the same value of $T$.
